attackModes.name: Single crack
attackModes.summary: Default first pass. Mangles GECOS / username fields with rules to catch passwords derived from account metadata.
attackModes.useWhen: Always the cheapest first attempt against /etc/shadow style files where usernames are present.

attackModes.name: Wordlist
attackModes.summary: Tries each line of a wordlist as a candidate. Add --rules to apply mangling rules (case, digits, leetspeak).
attackModes.useWhen: You have a plausible wordlist (rockyou, target-specific). The bread-and-butter mode.

attackModes.name: Incremental
attackModes.summary: Markov-model brute force using character frequencies trained on real password corpora. Modes: ASCII, Alpha, Digits, LM_ASCII, etc.
attackModes.useWhen: Wordlist + rules came up empty and you have CPU time to burn. Effective for short passwords.

attackModes.name: Mask
attackModes.summary: Targeted brute force over a known structure. Charsets: ?l lower, ?u upper, ?d digit, ?s symbol, ?a all printable.
attackModes.useWhen: You know the policy (e.g. 1 upper + 4 lower + 2 digits) and want to enumerate it directly.

attackModes.name: External
attackModes.summary: Run a custom candidate generator written in John's C-like external language (defined in john.conf).
attackModes.useWhen: You need a generator the built-in modes don't cover — e.g. dates, license-plate patterns, structured tokens.

commonFlags.description: Force a specific hash format (e.g. NT, sha512crypt, bcrypt). Bypasses auto-detect.
Print every hash format the build supports — useful for picking the right --format value.
Read candidates from FILE, one per line.
Apply mangling rules (default = Wordlist; jumbo adds KoreLogic, OneRuleToRuleThemAll, etc.).
Mask attack with charsets ?l ?u ?d ?s ?a or custom ?1..?9 sets.
Markov-model brute force; MODE selects the trained character set.
Name the session so you can pause and --restore=NAME later.
Resume a previously-saved session from where it stopped.
Print already-cracked passwords from the pot file (no further cracking).
Print only the hashes John has not yet cracked.
Use a custom pot file instead of ~/.john/john.pot. Handy for keeping engagements separate.
Run N parallel processes across CPU cores.
OpenCL / GPU device selection in jumbo builds with GPU support.
Only crack the listed users (or UID ranges).
Only crack accounts in the listed groups.
Benchmark every supported format. Quick sanity check that the binary is healthy.

extractors.use: Merge /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow into a single file John can ingest.
Extract crackable hashes from password-protected ZIP archives.
Extract hashes from RAR archives (RAR3 and RAR5).
Extract the encryption hash from a password-protected PDF.
Hash extraction for legacy and OOXML Microsoft Office documents.
Extract a hash from a KeePass .kdbx database for offline cracking.
Extract the hash protecting an OpenSSH private key passphrase.
Extract the encryption hash from a password-protected 7-Zip archive.
Extract a hash from a BitLocker-encrypted volume.

recipes.title: Crack /etc/shadow with rockyou + rules
recipes.details: The standard Linux engagement workflow: merge passwd+shadow, then run the rockyou wordlist with default mangling rules. Use --rules=Jumbo for a much bigger ruleset on jumbo builds.

recipes.title: Crack a password-protected ZIP
recipes.details: Every *2john helper writes a single line in John's hash format. From there it's a normal wordlist run.

recipes.title: NTLM hashes from a Windows dump
recipes.details: Force --format=NT so John doesn't misidentify short NT hashes as LM. Pair with secretsdump.py / Mimikatz output.

recipes.title: Mask attack with a known policy
recipes.details: Enumerates passwords like 'Hunter12' — one upper, five lower, two digits. Far faster than incremental when you know the policy.

recipes.title: Resume an interrupted session
recipes.details: Always name long sessions with --session= so you can pause overnight and pick up exactly where you left off.

recipes.title: Show what's already cracked
recipes.details: Reads ~/.john/john.pot and prints every hash from the file that has been recovered. Use --show=left for the unrecovered ones.

recipes.title: Benchmark a build
recipes.details: Spins each supported format for 10 seconds and reports c/s. Quick sanity check after compiling jumbo or installing a new GPU.

recipes.title: Hand off to hashcat
recipes.command:
john --show --format=sha512crypt hashes.txt > cracked.txt
hashcat -m 1800 -a 0 hashes.txt rockyou.txt --rules=best64.rule
recipes.details: Common pattern: John's auto-detection + format flexibility, hashcat's GPU speed. Same hash files; different runners.

configFiles.purpose: Pot file — every cracked (hash, plaintext) pair is appended here. Persists across runs.
Session log: speeds, candidate counts, key-rate samples. Useful for tuning rules.
Resume state for the most recent session. Per-session files when --session=NAME is set.
Main config — rule sets, charsets, external programs, defaults.

tips: Always run --single first on shadow files. It's near-free and catches passwords like the username.
On jumbo builds, --rules=Jumbo or --rules=All is dramatically larger than the default Wordlist ruleset.
Keep separate pot files per engagement with --pot=case-XYZ.pot. Pot pollution leaks data between cases.
Sort and dedupe wordlists first — duplicate candidates waste cycles. `awk '!seen[$0]++' raw.txt > clean.txt`.
Auto-detect can pick the wrong format when hashes are ambiguous (e.g. raw MD5 vs NT). When in doubt, set --format= explicitly.
Use --fork=N to scale across CPU cores (jumbo). For real GPU speeds, hand off to hashcat or use OpenCL formats in jumbo.
Track progress with the spacebar — John prints current speed, ETA, and last candidate without interrupting the run.
limitations: GPU support exists in jumbo via OpenCL but lags hashcat in coverage and raw speed — hashcat is usually faster for big GPU jobs.
Auto-detect can misidentify short or unsalted hashes; force --format= when the result looks wrong.
Some niche formats only exist in the community jumbo build (`john-jumbo`); the upstream Openwall release is more conservative.
Mask and incremental modes still have a hard wall: a strong, long, unique passphrase is functionally uncrackable offline.
Cracking hashes you don't own is illegal almost everywhere. Use only on systems and dumps you have written authorisation to test.
